scenario_description: Three bowls of batter (vanilla, chocolate, strawberry) are introduced. Only the chocolate batter is processed into a pancake and stacked with syrup, while the others remain untouched in subsequent shots.

turns.text: Here are the three batters I mixed up this morning.
turns.image_description: A top-down shot of a wooden kitchen counter. Three identical white ceramic bowls are arranged in a horizontal row. The left bowl contains pale yellow vanilla batter with small specks of lemon zest. The center bowl holds rich, dark brown chocolate batter. The right bowl contains pink strawberry batter with visible chunks of fruit. Natural morning light illuminates the scene from the left.

turns.text: Those look delicious! You have a nice variety there. Which one are you planning to cook first?
I'm starting with the middle one. Here it is going onto the griddle.
turns.image_description: A close-up side view of a black cast-iron skillet on a stove. A ladle is pouring thick, dark brown liquid batter from above into the center of the hot pan. Steam rises slightly. In the blurred background, the two other bowls (pale yellow and pink) are still sitting on the counter, untouched.

turns.text: That dark color suggests the chocolate one, right? Looks like it's cooking up nicely.
Yeah, that was the one. Check out the final stack I made.
turns.image_description: A high-angle shot of a tall stack of four fluffy pancakes on a white plate. The pancakes are a uniform dark brown color. They are topped with a generous pool of amber maple syrup and a pat of melting butter. No fruit chunks or yellow tones are visible in the stack.

turns.text: Wow, that looks perfect. The syrup really makes it pop. Did you use any toppings besides the butter?
Just butter and syrup for now. But wait, I need to double-check something about my workflow. Looking at the whole sequence, which specific flavor was the one that got poured into the pan?

ground_truth.reasoning_chain: 1. Turn 1 establishes three distinct entities: Vanilla (left, pale yellow), Chocolate (center, dark brown), Strawberry (right, pink). 2. Turn 3 shows the 'middle one' being poured; visually, this corresponds to the dark brown batter identified as Chocolate in Turn 1. 3. Turn 5 confirms the result is a dark brown stack, consistent with Chocolate. 4. The user asks which flavor was poured. By linking the spatial reference 'middle one' in Turn 3 to the visual identity in Turn 1, the answer is Chocolate. Options A and C correspond to the other two bowls which were explicitly shown remaining untouched in the background of Turn 3.
ground_truth.key_difficulty: The model must track the spatial position ('middle') from Turn 1 to Turn 3 and map it to the specific flavor identity established in Turn 1, while ignoring the distractor flavors that remained static. A model focusing only on Turn 5 would see a brown pancake but might not know if it was chocolate or a burnt vanilla without the cross-turn context of the initial setup and the pouring action.

_meta.scenario: Three distinct bowls of batter are shown: one vanilla, one chocolate, and one strawberry. In subsequent images, only the chocolate batter is poured into a pan, cooked into a pancake, and stacked with syrup, while the other two batters sit untouched.
